word_list.word: you can lead a horse to water, but you cannot make (him|it|her) drink
word_list.pronunciation: juː kæn lˈiːd ɐ hˈɔːɹs tə wˈɔːɾɚ bˌʌt juː kænˈɑːt mˌeɪk hˌɪm ɪt hɜː dɹˈɪŋk

senses.definition: used to suggest that while one can provide someone with an opportunity, one cannot force them to take advantage of it, as ultimately people must make their own choices and decisions

word_list.word: above black there is no color (, and above salt there is no savor|)
word_list.pronunciation: əbˌʌv blˈæk ðɛɹ ɪz nˈoʊ kˈʌlɚ ænd əbˌʌv sˈɑːlt ðɛɹ ɪz nˈoʊ sˈeɪvɚ

senses.definition: used to imply that when a situation has reached a certain extreme or chaotic state, it cannot become any worse or more aggravated

examples.x: The political situation had become so unstable that people felt that above black there is no color, and that the country was on the brink of collapse.

word_list.word: every man (has|hath) a fool in his sleeve
word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛvɹi mˈæn hɐz ɔːɹ hæθ ɐ fˈuːl ɪn hɪz slˈiːv

senses.definition: used to imply that regardless of how intelligent or wise a person may appear, everyone is capable of making mistakes or acting in a foolish manner at times

examples.x: Even the most successful businessmen have made foolish decisions at some point in their careers, proving that every man has a fool in his sleeve.

word_list.pronunciation: fˈuːlz mˈeɪ mˌaɪt sˈʌmtaɪmz spˈiːk tə ðə pˈɜːpəs pˈɔɪnt

senses.definition: used to suggest that even those considered foolish may occasionally have valuable insights or solutions to offer

examples.x: The supervisor had always dismissed the suggestions of the new employee, but when she offered a unique perspective that solved a long-standing issue, he realized that even fools may sometimes speak to the purpose.

word_list.word: if things were to be done twice, all would be wise
word_list.pronunciation: ɪf θˈɪŋz wɜː təbi dˈʌn twˈaɪs ˈɔːl wʊd biː wˈaɪz

senses.definition: used to suggest that if people were given a second chance to do something, they would likely make better decisions, emphasizing the importance of learning from one's mistakes

word_list.word: (whatever|what) man has done, man may do
word_list.pronunciation: wʌtˈɛvɚɹ ɔːɹ wˌʌt mˈæn hɐz dˈʌn mˈæn mˈeɪ dˈuː

senses.definition: used to imply that if someone has accomplished something in the past, then it is possible for others to achieve the same thing, encouraging people to pursue their goals with determination

word_list.word: lightning never strikes the same place twice
word_list.pronunciation: lˈaɪtnɪŋ nˈɛvɚ stɹˈaɪks ðə sˈeɪm plˈeɪs twˈaɪs

senses.definition: used to express the idea that a highly unlikely event or occurrence is not likely to happen again in the same place or to the same person

examples.x: When their house was burglarized, Tom and his family were devastated, but their neighbors tried to console them by saying that lightning never strikes the same place twice.

word_list.word: a ragged colt may make a good horse
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ ɹˈæɡᵻd kˈoʊlt mˈeɪ mˌeɪk ɐ ɡˈʊd hˈɔːɹs

senses.definition: used to suggest that something or someone that appears unpromising or unremarkable at first may have the potential to become successful or valuable in the future

examples.x: Although the new employee lacked experience, the hiring manager believed that a ragged colt may make a good horse and decided to give them a chance.

word_list.word: a stream cannot rise above its source
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ stɹˈiːm kænˈɑːt ɹˈaɪz əbˌʌv ɪts sˈoːɹs

senses.definition: used to imply that someone or something cannot surpass their own limitations, such as their own abilities, resources, or environment

word_list.word: hares (may|might|can) pull dead lions by the beard
word_list.pronunciation: hˈɛɹz mˈeɪ ɔːɹ mˌaɪt ɔːɹ kæn pˈʊl dˈɛd lˈaɪənz baɪ ðə bˈɪɹd

senses.definition: used to imply that power and influence can be fleeting, as even the weak or small can have power over the strong or mighty when they are in a vulnerable state

examples.x: In the world of politics, it's important to remember that hares may pull dead lions by the beard, as even the most influential leaders can fall from grace if they become too complacent or lose the support of the people.

word_list.word: the age of miracles is (long|) past
word_list.pronunciation: ðɪ ˈeɪdʒ ʌv mˈɪɹəkəlz ɪz lˈɑːŋ pˈæst

senses.definition: used to emphasize the idea that people should not rely on miraculous or supernatural intervention to solve their problems but should instead work toward practical solutions

word_list.word: if something sounds too good to be true, it probably is
word_list.pronunciation: ɪf sˈʌmθɪŋ sˈaʊndz tˈuː ɡˈʊd təbi tɹˈuː ɪt pɹˈɑːbəbli ɪz

senses.definition: used to suggest that if something appears too beneficial or advantageous, it is likely false or deceptive, urging people to be cautious and use critical thinking skills to evaluate such claims

word_list.word: men may meet but mountains never
word_list.pronunciation: mˈɛn mˈeɪ mˈiːt bˌʌt mˈaʊntɪnz nˈɛvɚ

senses.definition: used to suggest that there are inherent boundaries or limitations that prevent certain things from happening

examples.x: Trying to explain why the two companies could not merge, the CEO told the board that men may meet but mountains never, referring to the significant cultural and organizational differences that would make integration extremely challenging.

word_list.word: you cannot lose what you never had
word_list.pronunciation: juː kænˈɑːt lˈuːz wˌʌt juː nˈɛvɚ hˌæd

senses.definition: used to imply that it is not worth being upset or anxious about losing something that never existed, emphasizing the importance of focusing on reality

word_list.pronunciation: juː wˈʌn kænˈɑːt sˈɜːv tˈuː mˈæstɚz

senses.definition: used to imply that dividing attention, loyalty, or effort between conflicting interests or allegiances will lead to a lack of commitment and failure or poor performance in all of them

word_list.word: you cannot get a quart into a pint pot
word_list.pronunciation: juː kænˈɑːt ɡɛt ɐ kwˈɔːɹt ˌɪntʊ ɐ pˈaɪnt pˈɑːt

senses.definition: used to imply that it is impossible to fit too much into a small space, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and respecting the limitations of a situation
used to imply that trying to make everyone happy is impossible, emphasizing that it is important to be true to oneself rather than constantly seeking others' approval

word_list.word: if wishes were horses (, the beggars would ride|)
word_list.pronunciation: ɪf wˈɪʃᵻz wɜː hˈɔːɹsᵻz ðə bˈɛɡɚz wʊd ɹˈaɪd ɔːɹ

senses.definition: used to emphasize that simply wishing for something is not enough to attain it, and that people must work hard and take action to achieve their goals

examples.x: The athlete wished to win the championship, but her coach reminded her that if wishes were horses, the beggars would ride and that she needed to train hard and stay focused to succeed.

word_list.pronunciation: ɐn ˈɛmpti sˈæk bˈæɡ kænˈɑːt wɪl nˌɑːt stˈænd ˈʌpɹaɪt

senses.definition: used to suggest that in order to function well or be successful, one needs to have the necessary resources, such as knowledge, experience, skills, or material possessions
